data.title: SOLZ: A Straight Forward Solana ETF
data.description:
As we approach the potential approval of US-listed spot Solana (SOL-USD) ETFs in mid-October, there are a variety of different ways traders may look to speculate on a positive outcome for the US market. One such product is the Solana ETF (NASDAQ:SOLZ) from Volatility Shares. Unlike most of the other products in the market, SOLZ is a futures-based ETF and has no direct exposure to the underlying asset. In this article, we'll look at the fund details for SOLZ, compare it to alternative products, and assess whether Solana could be an outperforming token in the Digital Asset market at this juncture.

Fund Details

Launched in March, the Solana ETF from Volatility Shares is a futures-based product that allows traders/investors to get exposure to the price of the SOL token through an easy-to-buy ETF wrapper. The fund has over $231 million in AUM which makes it larger than SOL-proxy products that have been in the market for a longer period of time.

Fund Assets $231.7 million
Shares Outstanding 9,070,000
Expense Ratio 1.15%
Fund Inception 03/10/2025

Source: Volatility Shares, as of 9/16/25

One quick note on the expense ratio: the 1.15% figure is currently discounted by Volatility Shares for the first year of trading and the net expense ratio is currently 0.95%. As of market close on September 17th, the fund shares have a net asset value of $25.54 per share and a closing price of $25.55. For all intents and purposes, this fund trades generally at or close to NAV. This is important because, as we'll see in the next section, not all SOL-proxies can claim this.

Peer Comparison

Despite the lack of a spot ETF currently, there are several ways investors and traders can play the Solana price in a traditional brokerage account. For the purpose of comparison, we'll be looking at SOLZ relative to the following:

  • Grayscale Solana Trust (OTCQX:GSOL)
  • Osprey Solana Trust (OTC:OSOL)
  • REX-Osprey SOL+Staking ETF (SSK)

Each of the SOL-based products above offers investors exposure to the asset, but they function slightly differently. This table below shows differences in market cap, fees, and structure:

Solana Exposure Expense Ratio Market Cap Structure Native Yield?
Solana ETF 0.95% $211.83M Futures Fund No
REX-Osprey SOL 1.40% $273.34M C-Corp Yes
Grayscale Solana Trust 2.50% $62.37M Closed end No
Osprey Solana Trust 2.50% $30.33M Closed End No

Sources: Seeking Alpha, fund managers

The big benefit of a fund like REX Shares-Osprey versus the alternative is it offers investors the ability to have exposure to staked-Solana for native yield. This is important because Solana currently has an inflation rate of 4.9% and staking yield rate of 6.8%. None of the funds mentioned offer shareholders the ability to redeem the assets underlying the fund shares. Given this, one would think the REX-Osprey fund has the best total return after fees. Yet, since the inception of that fund on July 1st, it is actually GSOL that is beating the rest of the pack:

Since July 1st, GSOL has had a 69% total return versus a 39% total return for SSK. At 55%, SOLZ has been the closest to the true performance of Solana, by far. The outperformance of GSOL and the underperformance of OSOL are due to the closed end nature of how those products are structured.

The chart above shows the year to date trend in premium/discount to NAV for both funds. Interestingly, they traded at relatively close premiums through the first four months of the year. That changed in May when OSOL started trading at a discount and GSOL continued trending toward a higher premium. The biggest reason why GSOL has outperformed SOLZ is simply because the premium has increased from essentially NAV break-even on July 2nd to a 13% premium as of July 17th. In my view, this makes GSOL a 'sell' relative to funds like SOLZ or OSOL and I've argued that several times in the past.

Solana Still Looks Good

As a digital asset worth exploring, Solana actually looks pretty good from where I sit. Last month, I argued that Solana would be the next big DAT winner and so far I think that thesis is holding up well.

Since my Solana article on August 28th, SOL has appreciated by more than 20%. This is better than Bitcoin (BTC-USD), Ethereum (ETH-USD), and Ripple/XRP (XRP-USD); each of which have larger market caps than SOL.

In the all important stablecoin race, Solana has the third largest supply of stablecoins minted on the blockchain. With the exception of Tron (TRX-USD) which trades a minuscule 7x circulating fees multiple, Solana is the cheapest of the major smart contract chains by circulating P/F with large stablecoin supplies:

At an average circulating P/F multiple of 237x in September, Solana is far cheaper than Ethereum or BNB Chain (BNB-USD) even though it has more daily active addresses, does more daily transactions, and is comparable in fees. To be sure, Ethereum has a larger stablecoin supply and processes more stablecoin transactions. Still, as an altcoin with a favorable setup, I think Solana is in a good position.

Summary

I'm of the view that now is a good time to maintain a long SOL position. I take that view for two reasons: first, Solana is a high throughput, fast, cheap chain that maintains a top 3 stablecoin footprint in the public blockchain space. Second, it's cheap relative to major peers and has a DAT bid that I believe is just getting started.

I wouldn't overweight it against something like Ethereum, but I do think it's a coin to overweight against Bitcoin. I've said for several months that I think Bitcoin's cycle top is either close to current levels or already in. As I see it, major alts like ETH and SOL are good hedges against that thesis. In the event I'm wrong about Bitcoin being at or near its peak, I suspect the large alts will outperform.

If I'm correct that Bitcoin is at or near cycle peak, I think coins with strong utility will weather the storm better than those that are purely speculative. I put Solana in the utility category. As far as playing the Solana bull thesis, I think SOLZ will work perfectly fine until there is a spot ETF in the United States. At that point, I would expect it to underperform. For those who want SOL access through a traditional brokerage, I'm going to initiate SOLZ with a 'buy.'

data.title: Intelligent Development Could Make Spyre Therapeutics Best In Show
data.description:
Spyre Therapeutics (NASDAQ:SYRE) is engaging in the clinical development of multiple antibodies with potential in inflammatory diseases. These antibodies have the potential to be best-in-class, but intelligent trial design and development decisions may mean the company could get away with antibodies that aren't quite best-in-class anyway. This article takes a look at SYRE's trials and financials.

What are these antibodies?

SYRE's antibodies target three validated targets, tumor necrosis factor-like cytokine 1A (TL1A), α4β7 integrin, and interleukin-23 (IL-23).

Anti-TL1A antibodies, like Roche Holding AG's (OTCQX:RHHBY) afimkibart, Merck & Co's (MRK) tulisokibart, and Sanofi (SNY) and Teva Pharmaceutical Industries' (TEVA) duvakitug, have already demonstrated activity in ulcerative colitis (UC) and Crohn's disease (CD), and are in phase 3 trials. Although if subcutaneously dosed, they appear to require dosing every two to four weeks, in some cases with an initial IV infusion.

Takeda Pharmaceutical Company's (TAK) anti-α4β7 integrin antibody, vedolizumab (Entyvio), is already approved for the treatment of UC and CD. It requires two initial IV infusions then subcutaneous dosing every two weeks, or continued IV dosing every eight weeks.

AbbVie's (ABBV) anti-IL-23 antibody, risankizumab (Skyrizi) is approved for the treatment of UC, CD, plaque psoriasis and psoriatic arthritis. In UC and UD, dosing regimes involve initial IV infusions, although subcutaneous dosing every eight weeks is possible following that.

SYRE's antibodies then are SPY001 (anti-α4β7 integrin antibody), SPY002 and SPY072 (two anti-TL1A antibodies for inflammatory bowel disease and rheumatic disease respectively) and SPY003 (anti-IL-23 antibody).

SYRE already has phase 1 pharmacokinetic data for SPY001, SPY002 and SPY072. That data shows a long half-life for those antibodies, which should facilitate maintenance dosing every three months (Q3M) or every six months (Q6M). For example, the SPY001 phase 1 data show a half-life of ~80 days. By comparison, vedolizumab has a half-life of about 25 days.

As for the SPY002, a ~75 day half life was demonstrated, which is over three times what is seen with first-generation anti-TL1A's. At least at the time of interim data from phase 1 work with SPY072, there wasn't quite a long-enough follow-up to provide a half-life, although the pharmacokinetics were said to be comparable to SPY002, and compatible with dosing two to four times a year. It should be noted there are other next-gen anti-TL1A's in development, which also have longer half-lives. For example, Xencor, Inc. (XNCR) believes it can dose its anti-TL1A antibody, XmAb942, every 12 weeks, and has initiated a study of the drug in UC.

For SPY003, SYRE only announced dosing of its first patient in a phase 1 trial in late March, and guided with Q2'25 earnings in August that it was on track to provide interim data in Q4'25.

Fending off the competition

Even though there are other competitor antibodies in development for UC, such as first-gen and next-gen anti-TL1A's, SYRE may have a trial design that gives it an advantage.

SYRE notes that Johnson & Johnson's (JNJ) VEGA study showed that combinations of biologic agents can produce next level efficacy in UC patients.

While VEGA shows a combination of an anti-TNF, with an anti-IL-23, provides impressive efficacy in UC patients, SYRE believes anti-TL1A's and anti-α4β7 integrin antibodies can provide even greater efficacy.

Rather than guessing which combination of SPY001, SPY002 and SPY003, would provide optimal efficacy in UC patients, SYRE intends to try three possible doublets in a clever trial called SKYLINE-UC. The first part of that study (Part A) involves each agent used as monotherapy, and can begin producing results in 2026. SKYLINE-UC initiated in May 2025, with SPY001 already having advanced from phase 1, into Part A of SKYLINE-UC. As for SPY002, it is set to enter Part A in Q3'25, with entry of SPY003 into Part A dependent upon the results produced in phase 1 work (recall interim phase 1 data is expected in Q4'25).

SYRE's current timeline for Part A results from SKYLINE-UC is 2026. Part B, which includes additional monotherapy data, but also three possible combinations of SPY001, SPY002 and SPY003, won't produce results until 2027. Nonetheless, I believe those results might be worth the wait, since the company has a good shot with its trial design of finding a regime, whether it is monotherapy or a particular combination, which provides some of the more impressive response rates in UC.

Further, beyond SKYLINE-UC, SYRE is also running the SKYWAY-RD study of SPY072 in rheumatic diseases (rheumatoid arthritis, psoriatic arthritis and axial spondyloarthritis). SYRE announced the first patient had been dosed in SKYWAY-RD in September, and confirmed a timeline for proof-of-concept data of 2026. That means SYRE will have multiple proof of concept readouts in 2026, possibly from three agents in Part A of SKYLINE and from three different rheumatic diseases in SKYWAY with SPY072.

Financial Overview

SYRE finished Q2'25 with cash, cash equivalents and marketable securities of $526.6M. For Q2'25, SYRE reported; R&D expenses of $40.1M, G&A expenses of $11.8M and a net loss of $36.7M. Net cash used in operating activities was $87.6M in the first six months of 2025. At the current rate of burn then, the company would run out of cash in about three years from the end of Q2'25, or about mid-2028. In agreement with that, SYRE expects its current cash to last into H2'28. Further, the company may be able to raise additional funds using its at-the-market program, under which it has $179.1M worth of potential stock sales remaining as of June 30, 2025.

SYRE's drugs were discovered by Paragon Therapeutics and in-licensed by SYRE, but the milestone payments are quite modest. For example, a $3M milestone is triggered on the first dosing of a patient in phase 2.

Thus far we have exercised the Option and entered into license agreements with respect to SPY001, SPY002, SPY072 and SPY003. Under the terms of each License Agreement, we are obligated to pay Paragon up to $22.0 million based on specific development, regulatory and clinical milestones for the first product under each agreement. As of June 30, 2025, we have incurred a total of $12.0 million of milestone fees out of a total maximum of $66.0 million in potential milestone fees across all License Agreements. ... With respect to the SPY002 and SPY072 License Agreement only, on a product by product basis, we are obligated to pay sublicensing fees of up to approximately $20 million upon the achievement of mostly commercial milestones.

Comments on the Paragon-SYRE license agreements, SYRE 10-Q, August 2025.

SYRE does have to pay royalties on sales to Paragon, although these are not even double-digit royalties. I believe SYRE has set up a pretty favorable deal then, that has gained the company access to what could be best-in-class, or near best-in-class antibodies. Given the advantages of the company's combination trials, any slight deficit in performance might be overcome by the efficacy benefit of identifying ideal combination therapies.

The Company will pay Paragon a low single-digit percentage royalty for single antibody products and a mid single-digit percentage royalty for products containing more than one antibody from Paragon.

Comments on the Paragon-SYRE license agreements, SYRE 10-Q, August 2025.

There were 60,400,960 shares outstanding of SYRE's common stock as of July 31, 2025. There was also 13.8M shares worth of Series A preferred stock, and 0.7M shares worth of Series B preferred stock outstanding as of June 30, 2025. The company has a market cap of $930.7M per share.

Risks

There are multiple risks to any long in SYRE, a few of which will be considered in this section.

Firstly, SYRE could report disappointing results from its phase 1 work with SPY003 in Q4'25, which would also mean it might not be able to test combinations containing SPY003 in SKYLINE-UC, meaning a sell off could be particularly pronounced.

Secondly, SYRE might report delays in enrolling Part A of SKYLINE-UC, which would make it less likely they meet their timeline of 2026 for the first proof of concept results. The same reasoning can be applied to SKYWAY-RD, delays in enrollment can hurt the stock.

Lastly, SYRE's competitors can announce compelling data of their own, leading to the stock selling off. Even developments involving therapeutics with differing mechanisms of action in UC and CD could cause a sell off in SYRE's stock.

Conclusions and rating

SYRE has got its hands on several antibodies with multiple validated mechanisms of action, potential best-in-class or near-best-in-class properties (long half-lives) and is planning to try different combinations of them in the SKYLINE-UC study. I find it unlikely SYRE can't come up with a very compelling regime for UC in the next couple of years thanks to this thorough approach. The company has the money to get to a result, including proof-of-concept monotherapy results in 2026, and has shots on goal in rheumatic disease as well thanks to its SKYWAY-RD trial. Given these factors, I currently rate SYRE a buy.

Since SYRE trades above cash currently, and it will burn through a good chunk of its cash as it heads towards results, I stop short of rating it a strong buy, because it could simply pull back just waiting for results.

data.title: DTD: Resilient Income Amid Market Volatility
data.description:
Many dividend ETFs such as the ones I previously covered are focused on delivering high dividends above everything else, and the result is often a bad fund. The WisdomTree U.S. Total Dividend Fund (NYSEARCA:DTD) is taking a different approach. The fund's investment universe is all of the U.S. listed companies paying consistent dividends, and the weights are chosen based on the proportionate share of the aggregate cash dividends each component company is projected to pay in the coming year: not on yields. The result is a resilient fund with strong risk-adjusted metrics, and I rate DTD a buy. The dividend yield is not stellar, but the dividends are still higher than average and very resilient.

In this analysis, I will use the Vanguard Total Stock Market ETF (VTI) as a benchmark for the U.S. listed stock market without the focus on dividend, to really assess if DTD's strategy is creating value for investors.

Dividend Selectivity as a Structural Positive

The market benchmark VTI is using a very traditional market-cap approach to constitute its portfolio. This results in a portfolio of 3524 companies. DTD, on the other hand, is much more focused with 837 companies, mostly "value" ones based on the selection rule that selects companies paying steady dividends, with large size and liquidity. The weighting methodology I detailed in the introduction naturally favors larger companies, but not as much as a strict market-cap approach.

Indeed, despite the smaller amount of holdings, DTD is more diversified than VTI. You can see it clearly if you look at the top 10 holdings in both portfolios: DTD's top 10 accounts for 25.38% of the portfolio against VTI's 32.94%. Details below:

Another striking difference is that the weight repartition in DTD is much more balanced , with no clear focus in top 3 holdings, something you will have with VTI: DTD's diversification level is higher.

If we look at the sectoral split, there are also notable differences linked to the focus on dividends. The most obvious one is the tech concentration, and I think it is a good example of how DTD works, and how different it is from other dividend focused ETFs. The Information Technology sector accounts for 15.51% of the portfolio, less than half of VTI's 36.60%. This is because many tech companies are growth stocks, and those paying dividends are not showing stellar yields. However, as DTD focuses both on size and dividend payment, it still has a significant weight on large tech companies, and given the current market dynamic this is a really good thing: not holding tech is an opportunity cost.

The lower weight in tech translates to higher weights on dividend heavy sectors such as Financials, accounting for 21.31% of the portfolio (vs. 11.50% for VTI) and Consumer Staples, 11.28% of the portfolio against 3.80% for VTI. Overall, DTD's portfolio is value driven while VTI's representative of market dynamics, is more growth driven. Usually, this results in a performance lag for the value driven fund in medium term horizons because since COVID, the market favoured growth companies. You will see below that this is not the case for DTD.

Here is a recap table about sectors:

Sector

DTD (%)

VTI (%)

Financials

21.31

11.50

Information Technology

15.51

36.60

Consumer Staples

11.28

3.80

Health Care

10.93

9.10

Industrials

9.33

12.70

Energy

7.39

3.20

Communication Services

6.32

2.00

Utilities

6.29

2.60

Consumer Discretionary

5.44

14.50

Real Estate

4.56

2.50

Materials

1.64

1.50

DTD is fully passive, but the rebalancing methodology, focusing on dividend expectations rather than past payments, is adding active-like elements in the fund, and this is why the portfolio turnover is high at 17% against 2% for the fully passive VTI. The usual problem with ETFs with high turnover rates is that they cut losers after they became losers, DTD's forward looking approach is avoiding this problem.

Financial Metrics

For an ETF with regular rebalancing, "high" portfolio turnover and niche positioning, I am surprised to see that the expense ratio is only 0.28%. Of course, it is no match for the giant cost efficient 0.03% expense ratio of VTI, but considering the rest of the ETF landscape, this is not a high expense ratio.

The difference in expense ratios is compensated by a difference in dividend yields. DTD is distributing a resilient dividend of 1.92% (not stellar, but very resilient), backed by a 30-day SEC yield of 2.09%. This is 0.78 percentage points above VTI, 0.53 percentage points if we take the difference in expense ratios into account.

Without much surprise, the focus on dividend paying companies, usually more mature than the average listed company, gives lower valuation metrics for DTD's portfolio: a P/E ratio of 21.1 and a P/B ratio of 3.6 against 27.1 and 4.5 for VTI.

This is not necessarily a good point alone, as if you read my previous analysis on dividend focused ETFs, you will find that they always have lower P/Es, but that they strangely are showing higher risk metrics than VTI. This is not the case for DTD. The fund's standard deviation is 14.23% (3 years), 14.56% (5 years), and 14.57% (10 years), consistently lower than VTI's: 15.30% (3 years), 16.40% (5 years), and 15.85% (10 years). The difference is even more striking if we look at both funds' maximum drawdown over the last 5 years: -14.68% for DTD against -24.87% for VTI. DTD's positioning is indeed resulting in a more resilient fund against market downturns.

Now, DTD is indeed less risky, fine, but is the risk-adjusted performance matching this dynamic? Short answer, yes it does. Long answer, let's look at both funds' Sharpe ratios.

For DTD, we have 0.68 (3 years), 0.76 (5 years), and 0.71 (10 years). For VTI, 0.89 (3 years), 0.70 (5 years), and 0.77 (10 years). Both funds are close, as VTI is showing stronger metrics on the short term, DTD on the medium term, and both funds are very close on the long term (even if VTI is slightly ahead). Overall, two different strategies that appear to be optimal. DTD is resilient, showing conservative returns and VTI is more aggressive. In both cases, returns are matching the risks taken.

You will find the actual returns below:

VTI is consistently above DTD, but DTD is also showing strong returns over all time horizons.

The only concern I have about DTD is its liquidity. The fund is 1000x smaller than the giant VTI with $1.4 Bn in AUM. This is not a bad thing and remains significant, but the fund's median bid/ask spread is wide at 0.15%. DTD is meant to be a long term investment, so a tight spread is not as important as with other ETFs, but 0.15% is not liquid enough, and adds to the premium in fees. Monitoring the spread to capture a low level before making a transaction will not be an option with DTD.

Sector Dynamics

DTD is mainly relying on Financials, Consumer Staples, Health Care, and Industrials: defensive sectors with traditionally high dividend yields. The appeal of this focus is dual. The first one is historical, you can see from the section above that it works in reducing risks while keeping appropriate returns. The second is the current economic environment with inflation ticking back up, mitigating the pace of interest rate cuts, but balanced by the slowing job market. Increasing the defensive part of your portfolio can be a good idea, and DTD is a good option to do so.

The high focus in Financials is especially making sense as the industry, especially large cap companies (91% of DTD's portfolio) have benefited from the high rate environment to boost their net interest margins and consolidate their credit health. Lower interest rates will make their balance sheet stronger, but slightly reduce net interest margins. However, interest rates are not expected to return to pre-pandemic levels anytime soon, meaning that the industry's margin will keep being above average.

DTD's positioning is a hedge against tech and growth heavy ETFs like VTI, benefiting from high growth perspectives but also high volatility and stronger exposure to shocks. DTD is focused on sectors with low correlation metrics and stable earnings (such as consumer staples and utilities), making it a good option to complement your VTI position, or a S&P 500 ETF, also heavily focused on large cap growth tech companies.

The defensive aspect of DTD is further strengthened by the 91% large-cap allocation (the rest is 7.5% mid-caps and 1.5% small-caps) meant to build a portfolio of companies with strong balance sheets that can resist market turmoils and geopolitical risks (an attention point is that large-cap companies are often multinational companies with stronger exposure to global trade dynamics, but DTD's focus on resilient companies seems to be mitigating this risk).

You noticed that I keep saying that DTD is a defensive ETF to complement your allocation to other, more aggressive ETFs, and this is because the strategy of the fund will never be able to capture growth from dynamic companies that are not paying dividends and can be a formidable source of returns without additional risk (clear when comparing VTI's risk adjusted returns with those of DTD). DTD can be a standalone investment, but I think the correlation point above and the defensive aspect of the fund will make it even more efficient to boost your risk-adjusted returns paired with a more aggressive growth fund.

Risks

DTD is a defensive fund, but that doesn't mean that it comes without risks.

As it is focused on dividend paying companies, it is dependent on regulations such as dividend tax treatments that both have an impact on your returns and how portfolio companies are valued.

It is also important to note the concentration on key sectors such as Financials or Consumer Staples: these sectors are defensive ones, but they are not exempt from potential sector specific shocks. The regular forward looking screening methodology of the fund does provide a buffer about the companies with stronger exposure to such risks, but navigating a sector wide shock might be an issue.

The key concern for me, as I mentioned before, is the fund's liquidity that is terrible. A 0.15% median bid-ask spread is not acceptable and is the dark spot in DTD's bright profile. When you think DTD, you have to think about bid/ask spread monitoring and accept that you will make your transactions based on the spread of the day.

Focused on large US companies, the fund is moderately exposed to domestic monetary policy and the health of the US economy. The fund's strategy is a clear hedge against such risks, but not an immunity. Interest rate shifts will have effects on the fund's portfolio (less than on the broader market, yes), and this will also introduce some volatility until the environment is stabilized.

Conclusion

DTD is a solid defensive ETF with moderate but very resilient dividend distributions to complement (or replace, if you are especially risk adverse) your total market exposure. On a risk-adjusted basis, DTD is as performant as VTI for a marginal expense ratio problem. It would be a perfect ETF if the liquidity was good, but this is not the case. It is a buy opportunity, which should fit most investors' portfolios.

data.title: Restaurant Brands International: No Visibility On AOI Growth Acceleration
data.description:
Summary

I gave a hold rating to Restaurant Brands International (NYSE:QSR) (TSX:QSR:CA) previously as execution risk and slowing unit growth offset brand progress. There are positives to be encouraged about, but I don't think it's sufficient to justify a buy rating since consolidated unit growth is still weak, Popeyes US is facing intense competition, and BK US plans have yet to translate into unit growth. As such, I reiterate my hold rating.

Strategic Initiatives Gain Traction

Although I am not yet upgrading my rating, I think there are positive developments, which are becoming harder to ignore. The most encouraging data point comes from Tim Hortons, which accounts for ~43% of QSR's adjusted operating income [AOI]. In my previous note, I mentioned that operational improvements and a return to unit growth in Canada were positive signs, and encouragingly, we continue to see this playing out with 2Q25's results validating this-Canadian comparable sales growth accelerated to 3.6% y/y. Importantly, the growth was balanced between check and traffic, with positive sales across all dayparts, including a 5% y/y increase in the crucial morning segment. I believe the improvement is organic because it was driven by well-executed marketing campaigns, including the "Scrambled Eggs Loaded Breakfast Box" in partnership with Ryan Reynolds, the return of the popular item "filled Timbits," and also the launch of a summer cold beverage, including the new "quencher" flavors. All of these are organic drivers that QSR can repeat; we know that they are resonating with consumers not only because they have helped to drive >10% y/y growth in breakfast food sales in 2Q25, but also because guest satisfaction scores have reached their highest levels since tracking of this metric began in 2018.

At Burger King [BK] US, the 'Reclaim the Flame' plan is also showing clearer signs that it can work. While I had previously flagged franchisee buy-in as a key execution risk for the capital-intensive remodels, it appears that I have underestimated franchisees' appetite and the potential uplift in sales from this plan. If we look at the most recent numbers, company-owned stores show that remodels can boost sales, which, from the franchisees' perspective, means more profits, higher return on investments, and quicker payback. The bull case here is that this should drive franchisees to move forward with this plan, which should drive up overall sales in time.

Shifting focus to the international business, which contributes 26% of QSR's AOI, is also showing signs of growth inflection. The focus here is BK China, which was the example I cited previously about franchisee risk. But fast forward to today, 3 months later, I believe the narrative has now completely flipped. The segment delivered strong sales growth of ~10% y/y, with comparable sales up 4.2% y/y, and importantly, was due to the efforts of management. For recap, since taking control in February, management has installed new leadership, refocused marketing, and driven a return to positive same-store sales in 2Q25 (which was a recovery that management had originally expected only later in 2025 or into 2026). This not only de-risks a key growth market but also serves as a strong testament to QSR's ability to fix its business with the right execution.

But execution hurdles and demand headwinds remain an overhang

Despite the encouraging progress, there are still several near-term headwinds, which makes it difficult to turn bullish. The primary headwind, which I have also flagged in my previous write-up, remains the pace of unit development. While management remains committed to its long-term plan of reaching >5% net restaurant growth, I just don't see a path to this happening anytime soon when consolidated net restaurant growth continues to decelerate, coming in at 2.9% y/y in 2Q25. As for the Burger King US system, there is still no evidence to fully convince me that the new plan can drive more unit growth since it also continues to shrink, with a 1.2% y/y decline in its store count. While the international segment's unit saw positive growth, at 5.4% y/y, it has materially slowed from 8.2% y/y in 2Q24. It just seems like the FY28 target for 5% is hard to imagine as of today, and I expect that to limit the upside potential here.

My other concern is the Popeyes brand. Popeyes US, in particular, is still seeing negative comparable sales (saw a 0.9% y/y decline in 2Q25), and I don't expect a turnaround anytime soon since the cost environment is extremely intense, where rising beef prices have driven burger-focused peers to lean heavily into chicken promotions (per the 2Q25 earnings call). While Popeyes has a strong brand equity and a significant international growth runway, the reality today is that the domestic business is facing pressure, and this is another layer that limits growth potential.

Valuation

Put together, I think my view is that QSR is a mixed bag of positives and negatives, and the market is leaning towards negative because the fact of the matter is as of the latest results, comparable sales are still at low single digits, unit growth is not improving, and the fastest-growing brand-Popeyes-is facing intense competition that caused system-wide sales to collapse from >10% to just ~1.5%. You can see that consensus has cut their estimates since the earnings, and that has pushed down the share price and valuation (from ~18x to 16x NTM PE now), I believe. I do appreciate the progress made in Tim Hortons Canada and BK US; they are really encouraging, but until QSR fixes Popeyes and accelerates store growth, I doubt the market will turn positive on this stock.

Conclusion

My rating for QSR is hold. I like the progress with Tim Hortons Canada, BK US, and BK International, but as I said, unit growth remains below the >5% target, and Popeyes is facing huge domestic pressure from competition, which doesn't seem to be going away anytime soon. All in all, I think there is little visibility as to when AOI growth will accelerate, and for that reason, I think it is best to maintain a hold rating on the stock.

data.title: Philadelphia Fed Manufacturing Index: Highest Level Since January
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The Philly Fed's Manufacturing Business Outlook Survey is a monthly survey of about 250 manufacturers in the Third Federal Reserve District, which covers eastern Pennsylvania, southern New Jersey, and Delaware. Participants of the survey indicate the relative level of general business conditions in the region.

Since this is a diffusion index, negative readings indicate contraction, positive ones indicate expansion. While it focuses exclusively on business in this district, this regional survey gives a reliable direction of the broader Chicago Fed's National Activity Index.

The latest Philadelphia Fed manufacturing index showed activity picked up in September, reaching its highest level since January. The index rose 23.5 points to 23.2 this month. The latest reading was better than the forecast of 1.7.

Here is the summary from the survey:

Manufacturing activity in the region expanded overall, according to the firms responding to the September Manufacturing Business Outlook Survey. The survey’s indicators for current general activity, new orders, and shipments all rose, with the former two returning to positive territory. The employment index remained mostly unchanged and continued to reflect overall increases in employment. Both price indexes moderated but remain elevated. The survey’s future indicators suggest widespread expectations for growth over the next six months.

The first chart below gives us a look at this diffusion index since 2000, which shows us how it has behaved in proximity to the three 21st century recessions. The green dots show the indicator itself, which is quite noisy. Therefore, we've included the three-month moving average (purple line), which is more useful as an indicator of coincident economic activity.

We can see longer and deeper periods of contraction during each of the recessions, with shallower contractions in '02, '03, '11, '12, '13, and '15. More recently, the index's 3-month moving average contracted from July 2022 to December 2023; however, no recession was called during that time.

In the next chart, we see the complete series, which dates from May 1960. For proof of the high volatility of the headline indicator, note that the average absolute monthly change across this data series is 8.2.

The next chart is an overlay of the General Activity Index and the Future General Activity Index — the outlook six months ahead.

Firms Continue to Expect Growth

The diffusion index for future general activity rose 7 points to 31.5 in September (see Chart 1), its highest reading since May. The share of firms expecting increases in activity over the next six months (52 percent) exceeded the share expecting decreases (21 percent); 27 percent expect no change. The future new orders index edged up 3 points to 42.4, while the shipments index fell 9 points to 31.0. Expectations for employment were more widespread as the future employment index rose from 12.7 to 23.7. The future capital expenditures index fell 26 points to 12.5.

For comparison, here is the latest ISM Manufacturing survey.

Let's compare all five regional manufacturing indicators. Here is a three-month moving average overlay of each since 2004 (for those with data).

Here is the same chart including the average of the five. Readers will notice the range in expansion and contraction between all regions.

ETFs associated with industrials and manufacturing include the First Trust Industrials/Producer Durables AlphaDEX Fund (FXR), Industrial Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLI), Vanguard Industrials ETF (VIS), and iShares U.S. Industrials ETF (IYJ).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Tracking William Nygren's Harris Associates Portfolio - Q2 2025 Update
data.description:
This article is part of a series that provides an ongoing analysis of the changes made to Harris Associates 13F stock portfolio on a quarterly basis. It is based on Harris Associates’ regulatory 13F Form filed on 08/14/2025. William Nygren’s 13F portfolio value increased from $59.55B to $61.50B this quarter. The portfolio is diversified with recent 13F reports showing around 200 positions. There are 43 securities that are significantly large (more than ~1% of the portfolio each) and they are the focus of this article. The largest five stakes are Alphabet, Capital One Financial, Charles Schwab, Intercontinental Exchange, and Airbnb. They add up to ~18% of the portfolio. Please visit our Tracking William Nygren’s Harris Associates Portfolio series to get an idea of their investment philosophy and our last update for the fund’s moves during Q1 2025.

Harris Associates currently has ~$100B under management. Their flagship mutual funds are the Oakmark Fund (MUTF:OAKMX) incepted in 1991 and the Oakmark International (MUTF:OAKIX) incepted in 1992. Both funds have produced substantial alpha during their lifetimes: 12.78% annualized return compared to 10.70% for the S&P 500 index for OAKMX and 8.7% annualized return for OAKIX compared to 6.4% annualized return for the MSCI World ex-US Index. The other mutual funds in the group are Oakmark Select (OAKLX), Oakmark Equity and Income (OAKBX), Oakmark Global (OAKGX), Oakmark Global Select (OAKWX), and Oakmark International Small Cap (OAKEX). In December 2024, they launched the Oakmark US Large Cap ETF (NYSEARCA:OAKM).

Note: The top holdings in the Oakmark International Fund are not in the 13F report as they are not 13F securities. The positions are Glencore (OTCPK:GLNCY), BNP Paribas (OTCQX:BNPQF), Kering (OTCPK:PPRUF), Continental (OTCPK:CTTAF), Bayer (OTCPK:BAYRY), Fresenius (OTCPK:FSNUY), Ashtead Group (OTCPK:ASHTF), KB Financial (KB), and Daimler Truck (OTCPK:DTRUY).

Stake Increases:

Capital One Financial (COF): COF is a 3.48% of the portfolio stake built during the four quarters through Q2 2023 at prices between ~$85 and ~$122. The stake was decreased by 15% during Q1 2024 at prices between ~$124 and ~$148. Q4 2024 saw a similar reduction at prices between ~$144 and ~$191. That was followed by a ~17% selling during the last quarter at prices between ~$160 and ~$211. The stock is now at ~$230. There was a marginal increase this quarter.

Airbnb Inc. (ABNB): ABNB is a ~3% of the portfolio position established during Q4 2024 at prices between ~$125 and ~$147. There was a ~85% stake increase during the last quarter at prices between ~$116 and ~$164. This quarter saw a ~45% further increase at prices between ~$100 and ~$144. The stock currently trades at ~$126.

IQVIA Holdings (IQV): The ~3% IQV stake was built during Q3 2023 at prices between ~$197 and ~$232. There was a ~23% stake increase in the next quarter at prices between ~$177 and ~$233. That was followed by a ~11% stake increase during Q4 2024 at prices between ~$191 and ~$237. The stock is now at ~$190. There was a minor ~5% increase this quarter.

Note: They control ~6.5% of the business.

ConocoPhillips (COP): COP is a 2.83% of the portfolio stake primarily built during H1 2023 at prices between ~$94 and ~$125. The stake was decreased by 10% in the next quarter at prices between ~$99 and ~$123. Q4 2024 saw a similar increase at prices between ~$94 and ~$113. That was followed by a ~25% stake increase during the last quarter at prices between ~$87 and ~$106. The stock currently trades at $93.48. There was a minor ~7% stake increase this quarter.

Keurig Dr Pepper (KDP): KDP is a 2.53% of the portfolio position that saw a ~53% stake increase during the last quarter at prices between ~$30 and ~$36. The stock is now below that range at $27.20. This quarter saw a ~9% stake increase.

Elevance Health (ELV): The 1.45% ELV stake was purchased during Q4 2024 at prices between ~$364 and ~$507. There was a ~25% stake increase during the last quarter at prices between ~$365 and ~$439. The stock is now below those ranges at ~$316. There is a marginal increase this quarter.

Ally Financial (ALLY), Amazon.com (AMZN), Bank of America (BAC), Comcast Corp (CMCSA), Citigroup Inc. (C), Delta Air Lines (DAL), Equifax (EFX), First Citizens BancShares (FCNCA), GE HealthCare (GEHC), Merck & Co (MRK), Salesforce Inc. (CRM), Sysco Corp (SYY), TE Connectivity (TEL), Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD), and Willis Towers Watson (WTW): These stakes were increased this quarter.

Stake Decreases:

Alphabet Inc. (GOOG): GOOG is currently the largest 13F position by far at 4.91% of the portfolio. The bulk of the stake was purchased in 2014 at prices between ~$26 and ~$30. Next year also saw a ~20% stake increase at prices between ~$25 and ~$38. The two years through Q4 2020 saw a ~28% selling at prices between ~$53 and ~$91. The last three quarters of 2023 saw a ~22% selling at prices between ~$117 and ~$143. The stock currently trades at ~$252. There was a ~28% trimming over the last six quarters.

Charles Schwab (SCHW): The 3.41% SCHW stake was built during H1 2023 at prices between ~$47 and ~$86. The position was increased by 19% during Q1 2024 at prices between ~$61 and ~$72. There was a ~23% stake increase during Q3 2024 at prices between ~$62 and ~$76. This quarter saw a ~17% reduction at prices between ~$66 and ~$91. The stock is now at $93.28.

Intercontinental Exchange (ICE): The ~3% ICE stake saw a whopping 170% increase during Q4 2022 at prices between ~$90 and ~$110. The next two quarters saw another ~50% increase at prices between ~$95 and ~$113. There was a ~11% trimming during Q3 2024 at prices between ~$137 and ~$163. That was followed by a ~15% selling during the last quarter at prices between ~$142 and ~$178. This quarter saw a ~10% further reduction at prices between ~$147 and ~$184. The stock currently trades at ~$171.

Phillips 66 (PSX): The 2.61% PSX stake was built last year at prices between ~$106 and ~$132. The last quarter saw a ~20% stake increase at prices between ~$113 and ~$133. The stock currently trades at ~$131. There was a minor ~3% trimming this quarter.

Charter Communications (CHTR): CHTR is a 2.44% of the portfolio stake. It was established in 2015 when around 2M shares were purchased at prices between ~$150 and ~$190. Recent activity follows. Q3 2024 saw a ~20% selling at prices between ~$291 and ~$383. That was followed by a ~15% reduction during the next quarter at prices between ~$315 and ~$408. This quarter also saw a ~11% trimming at prices between ~$312 and ~$437. The stock currently trades at ~$262.

American International Group (AIG): The 2.40% AIG position saw a ~45% stake increase during Q2 2023 at prices between ~$50 and ~$57. There was a ~7% stake increase during Q4 2024 at prices between ~$70 and ~$78. This quarter saw a ~23% selling at prices between ~$76 and ~$88. The stock currently trades at $78.03.

CBRE Group (CBRE): The ~2% CBRE stake saw a ~20% increase during H1 2022 at prices between ~$70 and ~$109. H2 2022 also saw a similar increase at prices between ~$67.50 and ~$88. There was a ~12% trimming during Q3 2024. That was followed by a ~24% reduction during the next quarter at prices between ~$119 and ~$141. There was a ~10% trimming during the last quarter at prices between ~$120 and ~$148. The stock currently trades at ~$166. This quarter saw a minor ~6% trimming.

General Motors (GM): GM is a ~2% of the portfolio position. It is a very long-term stake. A small position was first built during 2007, but was disposed the following year. In 2013, a huge ~70M share stake was established at prices between $27 and $41. The position size peaked at ~81M shares during 2015. The stake was sold down by ~25% in the 2016-2019 timeframe at prices between $28 and $46. That was followed with a ~45% reduction in the five quarters through Q2 2021 at prices between ~$18 and ~$64. There was a ~25% stake increase during Q1 2022 at prices between ~$40 and ~$66. This quarter saw a ~15% selling at prices between ~$42 and ~$51. The stock currently trades at $59.26.

Deere & Co. (DE): DE is a 1.81% of the portfolio position established during Q1 2024 at prices between ~$356 and ~$411. There was a ~36% stake increase during the next quarter at prices between ~$364 and ~$412. The last quarter saw a ~13% selling at prices between ~$403 and ~$515. That was followed by a ~50% reduction this quarter at prices between ~$404 and ~$534. The stock currently trades at ~$476.

Centene Corp (CNC): The 1.80% CNC stake saw a ~47% increase during Q2 2024 at prices between ~$66 and ~$78. There was a ~17% reduction during the last quarter at prices between ~$55 and ~$67. The stock currently trades at $32.17. This quarter saw a minor ~3% trimming.

Fiserv Inc. (FI): FI is a 1.49% of the portfolio position primarily built during 2021 at prices between ~$96 and ~$111. The five quarters through Q2 2023 saw a ~40% selling at prices between ~$89 and ~$126. There was a one-third selling during Q4 2024 at prices between ~$181 and ~$223. The last quarter saw a ~38% reduction at prices between ~$200 and ~$239. The stock currently trades at ~$133. There was a minor ~6% trimming this quarter.

Nasdaq Inc. (NDAQ): The 1.13% NDAQ stake was established during Q2 2024 at prices between ~$59 and ~$63. This quarter saw a ~10% selling at prices between ~$65 and ~$90. The stock currently trades at $88.87.

Kenvue Inc. (KVUE): The KVUE stake was purchased during Q1 2024 at prices between ~$19 and ~$22. There was a two-third increase during the next quarter at prices between ~$18 and ~$21. Q3 2024 saw a ~7% further increase. There was a ~15% reduction during the last quarter at prices between ~$20 and ~$24. This quarter saw the position almost sold out at prices between ~$21 and ~$25. The stock is now at $18.10.

Bank of New York Mellon (BK), CNH Industrial (CNH), CoreBridge Financial (CRBG), Corteva (CTVA), EOG Resources (EOG), Kroger Company (KR), Paycom Software (PAYC), State Street Corp (STT), and Wells Fargo (WFC): These stakes were reduced during the quarter.

Below is a spreadsheet that shows the changes to William Nygren’s Harris Associates 13F portfolio holdings as of Q2 2025:

Source: John Vincent. Data constructed from Harris Associates' 13F filings for Q1 2025 and Q2 2025.

data.title: Main Street Capital: Overpriced And A High Premium To NAV
data.description:
Main Street Capital (NYSE:MAIN) is expensive. The business development company ("BDC") is trading at 2x its net asset value per share ("NAV") and offers an aggregate dividend yield that's only 240 basis points above the U.S. 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y). For some context, some of its peers, like Capital Southwest (CSWC) and Hercules Capital (HTGC), offer spreads of roughly 710 and 580 basis points, respectively. Crucially, these BDCs are also internally managed with investment-grade ("IG") credit ratings. MAIN was rated IG at "BBB-" by Fitch Ratings in June, with the credit rating agency highlighting the BDC's senior debt exposure and portfolio diversity as positive drivers. This is against a backdrop of headwinds from a more competitive underwriting environment in private credit and ongoing Fed rate cuts. I last covered MAIN in the summer of 2023, when I had a long position.

MAIN last declared a monthly base cash dividend of $0.255 per share, kept unchanged from its prior distribution, and $3.06 per share annualized for a 4.6% dividend yield. The BDC also distributes quarterly supplementals, with $0.30 per share set to be paid out in September. This meant a total aggregate distribution of $1.065 per share, around a 6.48% dividend yield. MAIN has been incredibly consistent with annual base dividend raises. There was a 2% hike in May, with the base dividend up by a 4.05% compound annual growth rate since the 2020 pandemic, when it paid out $0.2050 per share monthly. This is still around 37% lower than its peer group median. Hence, why is MAIN so expensive?

Record Base Dividend Yield Compression And The Fed

You could have maybe pointed to the monthly dividend schedule, but CSWC also pays out a monthly dividend, with some rumors that Trinity Capital (TRIN) is about to do the same. Is it the geography? These are all U.S. companies, primarily underwriting credit to other U.S. companies at either the middle market or venture stage. MAIN's expensiveness is not just on a comparative basis but also on a relative basis. The BDC's base dividend yield has compressed to its lowest level in well over a decade, eliminating any possible margin of safety for prospective investors just as the Fed is set to cut rates by a further 50 basis points through December after a 25 basis point cut in September.

The probability of back-to-back cuts to end 2025 currently stands at 82% on the CME FedWatch Tool. This is important, as MAIN expects net investment income ("NII") to dip by $12.17 million annually, around $0.14 per share, on the back of a 75 basis point decrease in the Fed funds rate. MAIN generated fiscal 2025 second quarter NII of $88.2 million, around $0.99 per share. This dipped by 2 cents from $1.01 per share a year ago. However, distributable NII in the recent second quarter was higher at $1.06 per share, providing roughly 100% coverage for MAIN's aggregate dividend.

Net Asset Value, Share Sales, And Credit Quality

MAIN reported an NAV of $32.30 per share, up by 27 cents sequentially and from $29.80 per share a year ago. Crucially, the BDC's ability to consistently grow NAV quarter-after-quarter and year-after-year forms a pillar for a premium to NAV that currently sits at roughly 104%. This premium means MAIN can accretively sell new shares, adding liquidity to its balance sheet and boosting its NAV. The BDC saw a 9-cent per share accretive impact from its stock offerings during the second quarter. Stock sales have added $0.58 per share to its NAV over the prior four quarters, placing this strategy as a core driver of NAV gains for a BDC whose book value has now risen in a near-straight line over the last decade.

MAIN's shares outstanding is up by roughly 35% over the last five years. The BDC also boasts a huge operating cost advantage over its peers, with operating expenses as a percentage of total assets for the trailing twelve-month period as of the second quarter less than half the level of the broader BDC space. However, there are risks on the horizon for MAIN that could see some near-term volatility with its common equity, especially as the Fed pushes through expected rate cuts.

The BDC's investments on non-accrual status formed 2.1% of its total investment portfolio at fair value, around 5% at cost, as of the end of the second quarter. This is elevated versus peers BDCs like CSWC and HTGC, both with nonaccruals of 0.8% and 0.2% at fair value, respectively. MAIN had $8.4 billion in capital under management as of the end of the second quarter, with $6.5 billion of this held internally at MAIN and the remainder, $1.9 billion, as the investment adviser to external parties from which the BDC earns fees. This asset management business forms a key differentiator for MAIN, contributing $8.7 million to NII during the second quarter.

Conclusion

MAIN's uniquely high premium to NAV is part of its investment pitch. The BDC has been able to consistently raise its monthly distribution and grow NAV for years and is set to continue to do so even as the Fed begins to moderate an elevated funds rate. However, I don't think MAIN is a clear buy now. The premium is just far too high from a relative perspective, especially against pending headwinds from the Fed. I'd like to eventually own the business again, but not for any price. MAIN is being rated as a hold for its current shareholders.

data.title: Rates Spark: The Long-End Bias Is Still Up
data.description:
By Padhraic Garvey, CFA, Regional Head of Research, Americas; Benjamin Schroeder, Senior Rates Strategist; and Michiel Tukker, Senior European Rates Strategist

Market rates are higher as the dust slowly settles in the 24-48 hours after Wednesday's Fed 25bp rate cut, and curves have re-steepened. Not exactly feeling like a bull market for bonds

Upward pressure on US market rates, while TIC data show ongoing reasonable support for US securities

As we settle in a day post the 25bp rate cut from the Federal Reserve, we find market rates higher and the curve steeper. Certainly Thursday's jobless claims and Philadelphia Fed data (both firm for the economy) pushed in the direction of higher rates, but the tone from the US close in the hours after the Fed decision on Wednesday was also pushing in that direction.

It was Asian and European trading that took the US Treasury yield back down from a possible attack on 4.1%. But that attack subsequently happened as the US trading day took control (especially post the data). So far, the 4% level has broadly held for the 10yr yield.

Latest Treasury International Capital (TIC) data show a moderate net inflow to US securities of some $2bn. The breakout shows selling of $25bn by China and $26bn by Japan, which has been thematic for both over previous years. There was also net selling of note out of Brazil, India and Belgium (custodian centre). The biggest net seller was Canada, at $57bn, reminiscent of the big sell trade done in April that was then reversed in May.

At the other end of the spectrum, the UK was a big buyer, at $41bn, as were many other European centres, the UAE and the likes of Hong Kong and South Korea. Excluding China and Japan, all other foreigners were net buyers to the tune of $54bn. In the past 12 months, average foreign buying is running at $61bn per month versus $134bn per month by domestics.

Overall, the July number as a whole, while not impressive, continues a theme of reasonable support for US securities. This helps provide underlying support seen for US Treasuries, but the data refer to flows through to end-July and so not timely enough to help explain more recent price action.

Long end still looks at steepening pressure, but the narrative is getting less straightforward

Both the German Bund and UK gilt 10s30s curve saw individual jolts steeper on the back of the funding calendar update and the Bank of England quantitative tightening announcement, respectively.

All in all, not the relief that some may have hoped for the very long end coming out of these events. Governments continue to issue on the long end, whilst the potential impact of a slowdown of QT programmes doesn't seem to change the direction.

Germany skewed the €10.5bn increase to the long end with 33% of the nominal increase in 15y and 30y maturities. Looking at it in terms of risk that the market will have to digest, the skew is at 58% via these longer maturities.

The BoE did largely match expectations by announcing a reduction of the gilt portfolio by £70bn over the next 12 months. As was largely anticipated, the active sales via the 20y or longer bucket were pared back, but not suspended altogether, as some market participants appear to have hoped.

Having said that, the narrative around the further steepening might be getting less straightforward with an eye to macro dynamics in the US. Closer to home, we also have the Dutch pension funds, which may still increase hedging going into the transition, together with demand for fixed receivers due to inflation indexations. Meanwhile, steepeners (partly in anticipation of pension fund unwinds in 2026) are a crowded trade, which could amplify swings in the opposite direction.

Friday's events and market view

No notable events on the data calendar after the UK retail sales in the morning. From the Fed, Daly will be speaking on AI.

Going into the weekend, we also have scheduled rating reviews of Italy by Fitch (BBB/Positive), Ireland by S&P (AA/Positive) and Greece by Moody's (Baa3/Stable). France is up for review by DBRS, which has the country on AA(high) with a negative outlook.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Powell Pivots, Fed Cuts Rates As Jobs Market Weakens
data.description:
By Bryan Cutsinger

The Federal Reserve lowered its policy rate for the first time since December 2024, even as inflation stays stubbornly above target. Signals show more cuts could follow.

The Federal Reserve lowered its federal funds rate target range by 25 basis points, to 4.00–4.25 percent, on Wednesday. Before the decision, it had held rates steady since December 2024. The move came as no surprise to market participants, who had widely anticipated a cut in light of the weakening labor market.

At the post-meeting press conference, Fed Chair Jerome Powell stressed challenges on both sides of the Fed’s dual mandate. Unemployment, while still low, has edged higher in recent months, and job growth has slowed with risks of further weakening. At the same time, inflation has picked up again and remains above the Fed’s 2-percent target.

Powell noted that real gross domestic product growth slowed to 1.5 percent in the first half of the year, reflecting weaker consumer spending. Business investment, however, has risen since last year. The median committee member raised its 2025 real GDP growth projection to 1.6 percent, up from 1.4 percent in June.

Powell reported that wage growth is moderating but still outpacing inflation. Job growth, however, has slowed sharply, reflecting lower labor force participation, reduced immigration, and weakening labor demand. At present, job gains are too modest to hold the unemployment rate steady. Even so, the median committee member left its 2025 unemployment projection unchanged at 4.5 percent.

Powell acknowledged that inflation remains above target and has picked up in recent months. He observed that expectations have drifted higher this year, reflecting concerns about the lingering effects of tariffs. Long-run expectations remain anchored near 2 percent, though. The median committee member continues to project 3.0 percent inflation in 2025.

Echoing past remarks, Powell underscored the uncertainty surrounding tariffs and their effect on inflation. While tariffs have pushed some prices higher, the broader impact remains unclear. He explained that tariff-driven inflation should be short-lived - what I’ve described as a one-time shift in the price level - but cautioned that the effect could prove more persistent if prices adjust slowly.

Powell explained that rising inflation risks and a weakening labor market have put the Fed’s dual mandate in tension. The committee judged that employment risks have intensified since its last meeting, making a rate cut appropriate. He also cast the move as a step toward a neutral stance, acknowledging that policy is still tight.

Judging by their projections, everyone on the committee appears to agree that the current federal funds rate target range remains above the longer-run neutral rate. The median committee member thinks the current target range is at least 100 basis points above neutral. The relevant question, then, is how quickly the FOMC should return monetary policy to a neutral stance.

Some FOMC members prefer a wait-and-see approach, at least in the near term. According to the Fed’s dot plot, seven committee members indicated the federal funds rate target range should be no lower by the end of the year. But most believe additional easing will be warranted this year.

Twelve committee members said the federal funds rate target range should be at least 25 basis points lower, and ten of those thought it should be at least 50 basis points lower. The median member now projects the midpoint of the federal funds rate target range at 3.6 percent following the December meeting, having previously projected it would decline to just 3.9 percent this year.

One committee member, most likely the newly appointed Governor Stephen Miran, thinks the FOMC should take a much more aggressive approach toward a return to neutral. That participant indicated the federal funds rate should be between 2.75 and 3.0 percent by the end of the year. Miran dissented from this week’s decision, preferring a deeper, 50-basis-point cut.

Taken together, Powell’s comments reflected a clear break from his past remarks about the health of the labor market and broader economy. With unemployment risks now taking center stage, the Fed has opened the door to further rate cuts, even as inflation remains above target.

The path ahead will hinge on whether the recent weakness in the labor market deepens and whether longer-term inflation expectations remain anchored near the Fed’s target.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Snap: Market Share Loser With No Profits
data.description:
Sometimes, investors get so caught up in deep analysis, digging into every tiny detail, that they skip the most important and simple aspects of making an investment decision.

Snap Inc. (NYSE:SNAP) is one of those companies that, if investors focused on the basics, they would probably be able to avoid meaningful losses.

With shares down over 70% from the 2017 IPO price and over 90% from their peak, I argue that Snapchat remains uninvestable.

Let's dive in.

The Snapchat Business

Founded in 2011, Snapchat came to the world with the ambition to transform the social media landscape, focusing on private photo and video communication between people who know each other. Several years later, Snapchat invented "Stories," which became one of the most popular features on social platforms.

In its early days, Snapchat shot out of a cannon, with user counts growing sharply. Then, as the company started focusing on things like augmented reality and camera technology, and after Instagram from Meta Platforms, Inc. (META) introduced its own Stories feature in 2018, growth has slowed materially. It did accelerate for a short while during the pandemic, but it has plummeted since.

The fluctuations in revenue growth almost perfectly correlate with the stock's performance since its IPO and reflect the company's failure to deliver on lofty expectations.

Today, Snapchat has 932 million monthly active users, growing at a single-digit pace year-over-year, with about 50% of those being daily actives. Snapchat is still quite popular among teens, but it failed to reach the masses worldwide like TikTok, Facebook, or Instagram.

In fact, Snapchat is experiencing a consistent decline in daily active users in the United States, as teens graduate out of the platform.

Like other social media platforms, and despite efforts to build AR glasses and a subscription business, Snapchat's main source of revenue, at 88%, remains advertising.

However, Snapchat monetizes its users at far weaker levels compared to Meta, with an ARPU that's about 80% lower at $2.87 compared to Meta's $13.7. On the contrary, it monetizes better than Pinterest, Inc.'s (PINS) $1.74.

Snapchat's advertising capabilities are far from the big leagues, especially after the introduction of Apple Inc.'s (AAPL) ATT, which made targeting and measurement much harder.

In my mind, Snapchat has only itself to blame for not being able to improve its ad stack and monetization, as the company hasn't exactly been scared to spend lots of money.

More Than A Decade Of Cash Burning

About 14 years after its founding, Snapchat is still dependent on investor money to fund its business, which is why shares outstanding have been growing consistently, and so has debt.

The company's gross margins of 51% are extremely low for its type of business. Meta and Pinterest, for instance, are around 80%. Meanwhile, its operating expenses are growing consistently, in line with or faster than revenue, as depicted by the lack of change in operational leverage.

Naturally, with no operating profit, there are no profits that flow down to net income, which has been negative since the company's founding. They turn to metrics like Adjusted EBITDA to show some type of cash flow, but stock compensation adjustments are the sole reason that's positive.

What is there to say, really? With growth decelerating and no sign of profits in sight, investors need to hang their hats on subscription revenues or an unforeseen transformation. Meanwhile, Snapchat is losing market share.

Management's Lack Of Focus Amid Consistent Market Share Losses

Snapchat defines itself as a "Technology company" that believes "The camera presents the greatest opportunity to improve the way people live and communicate."

The company invested more than $3 billion over the past 11 years in its AR technology and plans to make its "Specs" available in 2026.

To me, it's one thing if you're Meta and you make so much money you try to invest in the next frontier, in hopes of not being bound by another platform provider. It's a whole other thing if you're a money-losing business that has so much runway to improve its core business.

At a relatively small size with 1% market share, Snapchat hasn't been able to outgrow its larger peers. In fact, it lost market share in 2 out of the last 4 quarters, with growth slowing at a time when others are accelerating.

That is even worse, considering much of the growth is coming from non-advertising revenue lines, which I include in the numbers for the sake of methodological consistency.

Snapchat has a unique and quite dedicated user base, and the fact that they aren't working day and night to improve their ad capabilities would make me very frustrated if I were an investor.

Valuation

At $8 per share, Snapchat has a market cap of roughly $13 billion and an enterprise value of $14.4 billion.

The company isn't generating profits, so we cannot evaluate it based on a P/E multiple. Its free cash flow is also negligible, so we can't turn to FCF multiples as well.

We'll have to play along and rely on adjusted EBITDA. According to their outlook for Q3, management expects to see adjusted EBITDA in the range of $110 to $135 million. Taking the midpoint and annualizing it, we arrive at a run-rate adjusted EBITDA of $490 million.

This places Snapchat at an EV/Adj. EBITDA multiple in the high 20s, quite lofty for an unprofitable company that's expected to grow revenues at a high-single digit rate.

For context, Meta and Pinterest trade at 16x and 17x, respectively. Both are expected to grow at a 50% faster mid-teens pace. Needless to say, they are also both profitable, and for both we're talking about EBITDA, not adjusted EBITDA, which excludes over $1 billion in SBC in favor of Snapchat.

I don't know how you can look at these numbers and reach a conclusion other than that Snapchat is highly risky and overvalued.

Conclusion

Snapchat is closing in on 14 years of losing money.

The company is growing revenues slower than larger peers like Meta and Pinterest while being far behind in terms of ad capabilities, leading to consistent market share losses. User growth has slowed and even turned negative in North America.

As operating losses mount and net debt is rising, management is focusing on areas like AR glasses, which are far from contributing to business results.

All that, and Snapchat still trades at a lofty valuation compared to faster-growing peers.

Despite the terrible share performance, I still think there's significant downside and initiate Snapchat at a "Sell."
